html:
[216]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 暑夜納涼 - Means 'to cool off on a hot night,' indicating the setting and purpose of the action.
  • 窗半開 - Literally 'the window half open,' describing the state of the window.
  • 叉探身入 - Means 'to poke in with a forked body,' likely referring to a snake or similar creature.
  • 竟體純青 - Means 'the entire body pure green,' describing the color of the creature.
  • 躍近榻前 - Means 'leapt close to the bed,' indicating the creature's approach.
  • 以巨爪扼其肩 - Means 'with huge claws clutched his shoulder,' describing the creature's attack.
  • 深入肌理 - Means 'deep into the flesh,' indicating the severity of the attack.
  • 驚踣床下 - Means 'frightened and fell off the bed,' describing the victim's reaction.
  • 昏不知人 - Means 'lost consciousness and was unaware,' indicating the victim's state after the attack.
  • 及曉 - Means 'by dawn,' indicating the time of the next day.
  • 血涔涔遍背矣 - Means 'blood dripped all over his back,' indicating the extent of the injury.
  • 其弟 - Means 'his younger brother,' introducing the second part of the story.
  • 貿易都中 - Means 'trading in the capital,' indicating the brother's occupation and location.
  • 寓室逼側 - Means 'a cramped guest room,' describing the brother's living conditions.
  • 夜息燈獨寢 - Means 'at night, extinguished the lamp and slept alone,' setting the scene for the second incident.
  • 忽有光荧然自墙隅出 - Means 'suddenly, light flickered out from the corner of the wall,' describing the appearance of the creature.
  • 乃一巨蟒 - Means 'it was a huge python,' identifying the creature.
  • 头大如斗 - Means 'the head as large as a winnowing basket,' describing the size of the python's head.
  • 目灼灼类双炬 - Means 'eyes bright like twin torches,' describing the intensity of the python's eyes.
  • 肋下有翅如鱼 - Means 'wings under the ribs like fish fins,' describing an unusual feature of the python.
  • 逼近榻前 - Means 'approached close to the bed,' indicating the python's movement.
  • 势将吞噬 - Means 'about to swallow,' indicating the python's intent.
  • 某急 - Means 'the person panicked,' describing the brother's reaction.
  • 拔关而遁 - Means 'pulled the door and fled,' indicating the brother's escape.
  • 回顾室中 - Means 'looked back into the room,' describing the brother's action after escaping.
  • 犹如燃烛 - Means 'as if a candle was burning,' indicating the continued presence of light.
  • 惊病，遂归 - Means 'frightened and fell ill, so returned home,' describing the brother's aftermath.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 暑夜 - Refers to a hot summer night, a common setting in classical Chinese literature.
  • 纳凉 - The practice of seeking coolness during hot weather, often associated with leisure and relaxation.
  • 窗半开 - Describes a typical household setting, where windows are often left partially open for ventilation.
  • 叉探身入 - Likely refers to a snake or similar creature, which is a common motif in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 竟体纯青 - Describes the color of the creature, which is often associated with snakes in classical texts.
  • 跃近榻前 - Indicates the creature's approach, which is a common narrative technique to build tension.
  • 以巨爪扼其肩 - Describes the creature's attack, which is a common motif in stories of supernatural or monstrous encounters.
  • 深入肌理 - Indicates the severity of the attack, which is a common way to emphasize the danger in such stories.
  • 惊踣床下 - Describes the victim's reaction, which is a common way to show fear and panic.
  • 昏不知人 - Indicates the victim's state after the attack, which is a common way to show the severity of the incident.
  • 及晓 - Indicates the time of the next day, which is a common way to mark the passage of time in classical texts.
  • 血涔涔遍背矣 - Indicates the extent of the injury, which is a common way to show the severity of the incident.
  • 其弟 - Introduces the second part of the story, which is a common narrative technique to show continuity.
  • 贸易都中 - Indicates the brother's occupation and location, which is a common way to set the scene in classical texts.
  • 寓室逼侧 - Describes the brother's living conditions, which is a common way to set the scene in classical texts.
  • 夜息灯独寝 - Sets the scene for the second incident, which is a common way to build tension.
  • 忽有光荧然自墙隅出 - Describes the appearance of the creature, which is a common way to build suspense.
  • 乃一巨蟒 - Identifies the creature, which is a common motif in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 头大如斗 - Describes the size of the python's head, which is a common way to emphasize the danger.
  • 目灼灼类双炬 - Describes the intensity of the python's eyes, which is a common way to build tension.
  • 肋下有翅如鱼 - Describes an unusual feature of the python, which is a common way to add to the supernatural element.
  • 逼近榻前 - Indicates the python's movement, which is a common way to build tension.
  • 势将吞噬 - Indicates the python's intent, which is a common way to build suspense.
  • 某急 - Describes the brother's reaction, which is a common way to show fear and panic.
  • 拔关而遁 - Indicates the brother's escape, which is a common way to show the climax of the story.
  • 回顾室中 - Describes the brother's action after escaping, which is a common way to show the aftermath.
  • 犹如燃烛 - Indicates the continued presence of light, which is a common way to show the lingering threat.
  • 惊病，遂归 - Describes the brother's aftermath, which is a common way to show the resolution of the story.

[217]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 邑之奇人 - A phrase meaning 'a remarkable person of the town,' highlighting the protagonist's uniqueness.
  • 勇敢有力 - Describes someone who is brave and strong, a common phrase in Classical Chinese to depict heroic qualities.
  • 慷慨轻财 - Means generous and not attached to wealth, indicating a noble character.
  • 家产尽让弟 - Means giving away all family property to his younger brother, showing selflessness and filial piety.
  • 己惟取一金簪 - Taking only a single gold hairpin for himself, further emphasizing his simplicity and self-denial.
  • 余物不问 - Not caring about the rest of the possessions, reinforcing his detachment from material things.
  • 嗜远游 - Having a passion for traveling far and wide, indicating a desire for adventure and exploration.
  • 生平履迹所到，凡十三省 - Having traveled to all thirteen provinces in his lifetime, showcasing his extensive travels.
  • 买薄货异地售之，仅足糊口 - Buying cheap goods and selling them in different places, just enough to make a living, showing his resourcefulness and frugality.
  • 夜不投逆旅 - Not staying in inns at night, indicating his preference for a simpler and more adventurous lifestyle.
  • 择山林坟墓僻静处展被偃息 - Choosing quiet and secluded places in the mountains and among tombs to sleep, reflecting his preference for solitude and peace.
  • 枕下一短刀 - Keeping a short knife under his pillow, showing his preparedness for potential dangers.
  • 见圹边立一小人，高尺许 - Seeing a small person standing by the grave, about a foot tall, introducing a supernatural element.
  • 探首下视 - Peering down into the grave, adding to the eerie atmosphere.
  • 俄去而复返 - Going away and then coming back, creating a sense of mystery and tension.
  • 又引数小人来 - Bringing several more small people, suggesting a gathering or a group of supernatural beings.
  • 咸俯圹边，彼此指划，似相告语 - All of them crouching by the grave, pointing and gesturing, as if communicating, adding to the supernatural scene.
  • 冯抽刀跃上 - Feng drawing his knife and leaping up, showing his quick reaction and bravery.
  • 见圹外尚有十余辈，纷纷星散 - Seeing more than ten more small people outside the grave, scattering like stars, indicating a larger supernatural event.
  • 冯仍卧寝，至晓而去 - Feng returning to sleep and leaving at dawn, showing his calm and composed nature.
  • 远见二人对搏 - Seeing two people fighting from a distance, setting the stage for a dramatic encounter.
  • 近视，乃虎与人对立 - Upon closer inspection, it turns out to be a tiger and a man facing each other, revealing the nature of the fight.
  • 人以斧斫虎顶，虎以爪攫人肩，相持并死 - The man chopping at the tiger's head with an axe, and the tiger clawing the man's shoulder, both dying in the struggle, highlighting the intensity and danger of the encounter.
  • 盖非柴夫，即猎人也 - It was either a woodcutter or a hunter, providing a plausible explanation for the encounter.
  • 晚年小康 - Living comfortably in his later years, indicating a change in his lifestyle.
  • 不事远游 - No longer traveling far and wide, showing a shift in his priorities.
  • 与妻子别院居 - Living separately from his wife and children, possibly due to his new lifestyle or personal reasons.
  • 晚散步庭中 - Taking evening walks in the courtyard, showing a peaceful and reflective routine.
  • 有风门一扇，在身后随之行 - A door following him behind, creating a mysterious and eerie atmosphere.
  • 约十余行 - Walking about ten steps, setting the scene for the supernatural event.
  • 冯回顾不惊，门至故处不动 - Feng turning back without being startled, and the door returning to its original place, suggesting a supernatural phenomenon.
  • 盖狐鬼戏之也 - It was likely a fox spirit or ghost playing a trick on him, providing an explanation for the supernatural event.
  • 奇人多遇奇事，斯亦奇矣 - Remarkable people often encounter strange events, and this is indeed a remarkable event, concluding the story with a reflection on the nature of the protagonist's life.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 冯君熙 - The name of the protagonist, Feng Xiyi, a remarkable person known for his bravery and generosity.
  • 十三省 - Refers to the thirteen provinces of China during the Qing dynasty, indicating the extent of Feng's travels.
  • 金簪 - A gold hairpin, a personal item that Feng chose to keep for himself, symbolizing his simplicity and self-denial.
  • 薄货 - Cheap goods, reflecting Feng's resourceful and frugal lifestyle.
  • 逆旅 - Inns or guesthouses, places where travelers would typically stay, but Feng preferred to avoid them.
  • 山林坟墓 - Mountains and tombs, places that Feng chose to sleep in, reflecting his preference for solitude and peace.
  • 短刀 - A short knife, a tool that Feng kept for protection.
  • 圹 - A grave or tomb, a place where Feng encountered a supernatural event.
  • 小人 - Small people, likely referring to supernatural beings or spirits.
  • 柴夫 - Woodcutters, one of the possible identities of the man fighting the tiger.
  • 猎人 - Hunters, another possible identity of the man fighting the tiger.
  • 狐鬼 - Fox spirits or ghosts, supernatural beings that Feng encountered in his later years.

[218]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 白塔寺 - A temple; literally 'White Pagoda Temple.'
  • 揚青驿河干 - Refers to a location near the riverbank, possibly a specific place or a general area.
  • 積柴如丘 - Describes a pile of firewood as high as a hill.
  • 仙居其内 - Suggests that a deity or spirit resides within the pile of firewood.
  • 鄉人祈祷辄灵 - Indicates that local people's prayers are often answered.
  • 收生妪 - Refers to a midwife.
  • 肩舆 - A sedan chair carried by bearers.
  • 内室锦帐绣褥 - Describes a room with luxurious silk and embroidered bedding.
  • 绿蛾双蹙，红粉凝娇 - Describes the beauty of a woman, with her eyebrows furrowed and her face rosy.
  • 一举四男 - Indicates that four boys are born in one birth.
  • 尻际有小尾 - Describes a small tail on the buttocks of the newborns.
  • 啼声啾啾 - Describes the crying sounds of the newborns.
  • 美妇四五人 - Refers to several beautiful women.
  • 金盆 - A gold basin.
  • 肴酒 - Food and wine.
  • 黄豆升许 - A measure of soybeans.
  • 一生吃著不尽 - Suggests that the soybeans will last a lifetime.
  • 乌有矣 - Means 'there is nothing there.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 白塔寺 - A temple that may have historical or religious significance.
  • 扬青驿 - A specific location or a general area, possibly a village or a town.
  • 河干 - Refers to the riverbank, which may be a common place for certain activities or beliefs.
  • 仙居其内 - Suggests a belief in the presence of a deity or spirit, which is common in folk traditions.
  • 乡人祈祷辄灵 - Reflects the local belief in the efficacy of prayers, often associated with temples or sacred places.
  • 收生妪 - A midwife, a common figure in traditional Chinese society.
  • 肩舆 - A traditional mode of transportation, often used by the elite or for ceremonial purposes.
  • 内室锦帐绣褥 - Describes a luxurious living space, indicating wealth or status.
  • 绿蛾双蹙，红粉凝娇 - Describes the beauty of a woman, using traditional Chinese poetic language.
  • 一举四男 - Indicates a multiple birth, which is rare and often considered auspicious in Chinese culture.
  • 尻际有小尾 - Describes a physical anomaly, which may be interpreted as a supernatural or mythical element.
  • 啼声啾啾 - Describes the crying sounds of newborns, often associated with joy and new life.
  • 美妇四五人 - Refers to several beautiful women, possibly indicating a celebration or a gathering.
  • 金盆 - A gold basin, often used in rituals or as a symbol of wealth.
  • 肴酒 - Food and wine, often associated with feasts or celebrations.
  • 黄豆升许 - A measure of soybeans, which may be a traditional gift or offering.
  • 一生吃著不尽 - Suggests abundance and prosperity, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
  • 乌有矣 - Means 'there is nothing there,' indicating disappointment or the disappearance of something.

Proper Nouns

  • 白塔寺 - The name of the temple.
  • 扬青驿 - A specific location or a general area.
  • 河干 - The riverbank.
  • 仙居其内 - The deity or spirit residing within the firewood.
  • 邻村 - The neighboring village.
  • 收生妪 - The midwife.
  • 肩舆 - The sedan chair.
  • 内室 - The inner room.
  • 佳人 - The beautiful woman.
  • 美妇 - The beautiful women.
  • 金盆 - The gold basin.
  • 肴酒 - The food and wine.
  • 黄豆 - The soybeans.
  • 珠 - The pearls.

[219]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 豫之茂才 - Refers to a talented individual from the state of Yu (豫) in ancient China.
  • 館于湯陰 - Means 'stayed in Tangyin,' indicating a place where the person was lodging.
  • 遇亂奔歸 - Means 'encountered chaos and fled home,' describing a situation of fleeing due to unrest.
  • 暴雨驟至 - Means 'sudden heavy rain,' describing a sudden downpour.
  • 茅舍 - Refers to a thatched cottage, a simple dwelling.
  • 素識 - Means 'an old acquaintance' or 'someone known previously.'
  • 言其情 - Means 'told him the situation,' describing the act of sharing information.
  • 款李共飲 - Means 'invited Li to drink together,' indicating a social invitation.
  • 更深雨歇 - Means 'deep into the night, the rain stopped,' describing the time and weather conditions.
  • 要路奮擊 - Means 'blocked the path and attacked fiercely,' describing an aggressive action.
  • 格斗 - Means 'fought,' describing a physical confrontation.
  • 裸李衣 - Means 'stripped Li's clothes,' describing the act of removing someone's clothing.
  • 攒殴 - Means 'attacked in a group,' describing a collective assault.
  • 致毙 - Means 'caused death,' indicating the fatal outcome.
  • 弃尸水中 - Means 'threw the body into the water,' describing the disposal of the body.
  • 置衣帽于庙台上 - Means 'placed Li's clothes and hat on the temple platform,' describing the placement of the victim's belongings.
  • 始散 - Means 'then dispersed,' indicating the end of the attack.
  • 经此 - Means 'passed by here,' describing the passage through a place.
  • 访知 - Means 'learned from inquiry,' describing the act of finding out information.
  • 讼叟 - Means 'sued the old man,' indicating legal action against the old man.
  • 止于戚家 - Means 'stopped at a relative's house,' describing a temporary stay.
  • 附家中人 - Means 'spoke through a family member,' indicating communication through another person.
  • 阎君 - Refers to the Lord of Yama, the god of the underworld in Chinese mythology.
  • 炮烙刑 - Refers to the punishment of burning alive, a cruel method of execution.
  • 冤雪 - Means 'wronged and cleared,' indicating the resolution of a wrong.
  • 新鬼故鬼 - Refers to new and old spirits, indicating the search for recent and long-dead souls.
  • 厉魄 - Refers to malevolent spirits, indicating the presence of evil entities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 豫 - Refers to the state of Yu (豫) in ancient China, which is located in modern-day Henan Province.
  • 汤阴 - Refers to Tangyin, a place in Henan Province.
  • 豫之茂才 - Indicates a talented individual from the state of Yu, emphasizing his status and origin.
  • 遇乱奔归 - Describes a common scenario in ancient times where people fled due to political or social unrest.
  • 暴雨骤至 - Describes a natural phenomenon that could disrupt travel and daily life.
  • 茅舍 - Refers to a simple, rural dwelling, common in ancient Chinese society.
  • 素识 - Indicates a pre-existing relationship, suggesting familiarity and trust.
  • 款李共饮 - Describes a social custom of inviting guests to share drinks, a sign of hospitality.
  • 更深雨歇 - Describes the passage of time and the cessation of rain, setting the scene for the events to follow.
  • 要路奋击 - Describes an aggressive act, possibly indicating a supernatural or criminal element.
  • 格斗 - Describes a physical confrontation, which could be interpreted as a fight or struggle.
  • 裸李衣 - Describes the act of stripping someone's clothes, a violent and humiliating action.
  • 攒殴 - Describes a collective assault, indicating the involvement of multiple attackers.
  • 致毙 - Describes the fatal outcome of the attack, emphasizing the severity of the violence.
  • 弃尸水中 - Describes the disposal of the body, indicating a lack of respect for the deceased.
  • 置衣帽于庙台上 - Describes the placement of the victim's belongings, possibly as a ritual or symbolic act.
  • 始散 - Describes the end of the attack, indicating the departure of the attackers.
  • 经此 - Describes the passage through a place, indicating the route taken.
  • 访知 - Describes the act of finding out information, indicating the effort to gather details.
  • 讼叟 - Describes legal action against the old man, indicating a dispute or accusation.
  • 止于戚家 - Describes a temporary stay at a relative's house, indicating a place of refuge.
  • 附家中人 - Describes communication through a family member, indicating a method of conveying information.
  • 阎君 - Refers to the Lord of Yama, a significant figure in Chinese mythology and afterlife beliefs.
  • 炮烙刑 - Refers to a cruel method of execution, indicating the severity of the punishment.
  • 冤雪 - Describes the resolution of a wrong, indicating a form of justice.
  • 新鬼故鬼 - Refers to new and old spirits, indicating the search for recent and long-dead souls.
  • 厉魄 - Refers to malevolent spirits, indicating the presence of evil entities.

Proper Nouns

  • 豫 - The state of Yu in ancient China.
  • 汤阴 - The place name Tangyin.
  • 李志青 - The name of the main character.

[220]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 化犬 - Literal expression meaning 'transformed into a dog,' used to describe a supernatural or magical transformation.
  • 昼寝 - Means 'to sleep during the day.'
  • 青衣白带 - Describes the appearance of the person, with '青衣' meaning 'blue robe' and '白带' meaning 'white belt.'
  • 神色张皇 - Means 'frightened or agitated appearance.'
  • 造其庐 - Means 'to visit his house.'
  • 祈善顾 - Means 'to kindly take care of.'
  • 遂寤 - Means 'then woke up.'
  • 后院 - Means 'backyard.'
  • 生数子 - Means 'gave birth to several puppies.'
  • 腰间白毛，周匝如带 - Describes the puppy's appearance, with '腰间' meaning 'around the waist,' '白毛' meaning 'white fur,' and '周匝如带' meaning 'all around like a belt.'
  • 后身 - Means 'reincarnation' or 'next life.'
  • 稍长 - Means 'when it grew a bit older.'
  • 送于长生院 - Means 'sent to the Longsheng Temple' (a place for taking care of animals).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 邑朱某 - Refers to a man named Zhu from a certain town or village.
  • 长生院 - Refers to a temple or institution where animals are cared for, often associated with Buddhist or Taoist practices.
  • 梦 - Dreams are often interpreted as having supernatural significance in Chinese culture.
  • 青衣白带 - The description of the person suggests a ghost or spirit, as such attire is often associated with the supernatural in Chinese folklore.

Proper Nouns

  • 邑朱某 - The name of the protagonist, Zhu, from a certain town or village.
  • 长生院 - The name of the institution where the puppy is sent, likely a temple or care facility.

[221]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - Classical format for introducing a saying or commentary, often used in literary or philosophical contexts.
  • 聞丁某貪暴霸產，無所不為 - Descriptive phrase highlighting extreme greed, violence, and exploitation.
  • 死而化犬，固所宜也 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that the person’s fate is fitting, implying a supernatural or moral retribution.
  • 冥罰可畏已 - Phrase indicating the fear of supernatural punishment.
  • 偶投 - Means 'to occasionally lodge' or 'to stay temporarily.'
  • 掩扉獨坐 - Describes the act of closing the door and sitting alone.
  • 察察作響 - Vivid description of a sound, possibly indicating something moving or shifting.
  • 拍簸 - Describes a shaking or rattling sound.
  • 梁塵飛落，仆仆如烟 - Poetic description of dust flying and settling, likened to smoke.
  • 牢上盹睡 - Means 'to doze off while sitting.'
  • 倚闾立 - Describes a woman leaning on a gate or doorpost.
  • 缟衣素裙 - Refers to white clothing, symbolizing purity or simplicity.
  • 妆束淡雅 - Describes a modest and elegant appearance.
  • 清明雨 - Refers to rain during the Qingming Festival, a time of mourning and reflection.
  • 湿纸灰 - Describes wet ashes, possibly from burning paper offerings.
  • 良人一去不复回 - Expresses the loss of a loved one who will not return.
  • 尘埋玉色，酒涸金杯 - Metaphorical expressions of decay and loss.
  • 家家春色不开门 - Describes a scene of isolation and mourning.
  • 红垂树杪，绿拥篱根 - Poetic description of red fruits hanging from tree tips and green plants surrounding the base of a fence.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 醉茶子 - A pseudonym or title, possibly referring to a literary or philosophical figure.
  • 丁某 - A placeholder name for an individual, likely a historical or fictional character.
  • 清明 - The Qingming Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday for honoring ancestors.
  • 牢上 - Refers to a stable or a place where animals are kept.
  • 倚闾 - A traditional image of a woman waiting for her husband or son, often associated with longing or sorrow.
  • 缟衣素裙 - White clothing, symbolizing mourning or purity.
  • 清明雨 - Rain during the Qingming Festival, often associated with sadness and remembrance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 闻丁某贪暴霸产，无所不为 - The phrase is elliptical, with '无所不为' (nothing they did not do) summarizing the extent of the person’s actions.
  • 死而化犬，固所宜也 - The metaphorical transformation into a dog is a complex idea that requires careful rendering.
  • 冥罚可畏已 - The phrase is concise but carries a strong emotional tone.
  • 牢上盹睡，迷离间见道旁一矮屋，环以疏篱 - The sequence of events is vivid but requires careful ordering to maintain clarity.
  • 清明雨，湿纸灰，良人一去不复回 - The poetic lines are rich in imagery and require careful translation to preserve their emotional impact.

Proper Nouns

  • 丁某 - A placeholder name for an individual.
  • 陶生 - A name or title for the protagonist.
  • 平阳 - A place name, possibly referring to a region or city.
  • 盐贾 - A term for a salt merchant, indicating a social or economic status.
  • 陶某 - A name or title for the protagonist.

[222]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 捉鬼 - Literally 'catching ghosts,' a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature, often used to describe encounters with supernatural beings.
  • 夜醉歸 - Means 'returning drunk at night,' indicating the state of the protagonist.
  • 力牽其裾 - Means 'pulling his robe with force,' describing the ghost's attempt to drag the protagonist into the water.
  • 撐拒 - Means 'struggling against,' indicating the protagonist's resistance.
  • 勿為所迷 - Means 'do not be deceived by it,' a warning from the protagonist's friend.
  • 亡友 - Means 'deceased friend,' indicating the identity of the person who intervenes.
  • 亟求相助 - Means 'urgently seeking help,' describing the protagonist's reaction.
  • 亂發一團 - Means 'a clump of tangled hair,' the form the ghost takes after being caught.
  • 冥報 - Means 'retribution in the afterlife,' a concept in Chinese belief systems about punishment for wrongdoing in the afterlife.
  • 貪暴 - Means 'greedy and violent,' describing the character's personality.
  • 頂上血流不止 - Means 'blood flowing continuously from the top of the head,' describing the physical manifestation of the character's punishment.
  • 大杖力撻其頂 - Means 'beaten on the top of the head with a large stick,' describing the punishment.
  • 砰然血濺數步之外 - Means 'blood splattered several steps away with a loud sound,' describing the intensity of the beating.

  • The clause '请留姓字，熊大人将为请旌延僧超度，以慰贞魂，且为天下后世听讼不慎者戒' must be read as a request for a posthumous honor and a religious ceremony, along with a warning.

  • The clause '第汝前生以不白之事误我，我今日以不白之事误尔' must be read as a reference to a past life and a current wrongdoing.

  • The clause '熊准其请，遂旋里，妇不再扰' must be read as the completion of the resignation and the end of the disturbance.

  • 工人嗤其诬妄 - The workers laughed at it as false and absurd, describing their reaction to the diviner's claim.
  • 有二鲤鱼跃出 - Two carp leaped out, describing the unexpected outcome of the digging.
  • 惧而瘗诸其侧 - They were afraid and buried them beside the site, describing the workers' reaction.

  • 仆敢不纳 - '仆' is a humble term for 'I,' '敢' means 'dare,' and '不纳' means 'not to accept.' Together, it implies a polite refusal.
  • 乌用是貌貌者为哉 - '乌用' means 'what use,' '是' means 'this,' '貌貌者' refers to someone who appears normal or ordinary, and '为哉' is a rhetorical question. The phrase asks, 'What use is there in having someone who looks ordinary?'
  • 灵气所钟 - '灵气' means 'spiritual energy' or 'vital force,' and '所钟' means 'concentrated' or 'imbued.' Together, it refers to being blessed with spiritual energy.

  • 修炼成仙 - '修炼' means 'to cultivate' or 'to practice,' and '成仙' means 'to become an immortal.' Together, it refers to the process of becoming an immortal through cultivation.
  • 绸缪年余 - '绸缪' means 'to be intimate' or 'to be close,' and '年余' means 'for more than a year.' The phrase suggests being intimate for over a year.
  • 谪限已满 - '谪限' means 'exile term' or 'punishment period,' and '已满' means 'completed.' Together, it refers to the completion of a punishment or exile period.
  • 盛妆出门 - '盛妆' means 'dressed in fine clothes,' and '出门' means 'to leave the house.' The phrase describes leaving the house dressed in fine clothes.
  • 彩霞万道，从空堕地 - '彩霞' means 'rainbow-colored clouds,' '万道' means 'thousands of rays,' and '从空堕地' means 'falling from the sky to the ground.' The phrase describes a spectacular scene of rainbow-colored clouds falling from the sky.
  • 层层如丹梯 - '层层' means 'layer by layer,' '如' means 'like,' and '丹梯' means 'red ladder.' The phrase describes the layers resembling a red ladder.
  • 冉冉入云端而没 - '冉冉' means 'gradually' or 'slowly,' '入云端' means 'into the clouds,' and '没' means 'disappear.' The phrase describes gradually disappearing into the clouds.
  • 床头一巨卵如瓜 - '床头' means 'by the bed,' '一巨卵' means 'a huge egg,' and '如瓜' means 'like a melon.' The phrase describes a huge egg by the bed, resembling a melon.
  • 俄而自破 - '俄而' means 'soon' or 'in a moment,' and '自破' means 'spontaneously breaks.' The phrase describes the egg breaking soon after.
  • 有婴儿攒抱其中 - '有婴儿' means 'there is a baby,' '攒抱' means 'gathered in the arms,' and '其中' means 'inside.' The phrase describes a baby gathered in the arms inside the egg.
  • 方面大耳，貌殊不凡 - '方面' means 'broad face,' '大耳' means 'large ears,' and '貌殊不凡' means 'appearance is extraordinary.' The phrase describes a baby with a broad face, large ears, and an extraordinary appearance.
  • 遣乳媪哺之 - '遣' means 'send,' '乳媪' means 'nurse,' and '哺之' means 'to feed him.' The phrase describes sending a nurse to feed the baby.
  • 及长，智虑过人 - '及长' means 'when he grew up,' '智虑' means 'wisdom and consideration,' and '过人' means 'exceeding others.' The phrase describes the person growing up with wisdom and consideration exceeding others.
  • 善贸易 - '善' means 'skilled at,' and '贸易' means 'trade' or 'commerce.' The phrase describes being skilled in trade.
  • 时中青犯禁，私货之 - '时中青' is a historical figure, '犯禁' means 'violate the law,' and '私货之' means 'to smuggle goods.' The phrase describes the person smuggling goods during a time of prohibition.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 卿是仙人，苟冒然而来，仆敢不纳，乌用是貌貌者为哉 - This sentence is a series of rhetorical questions and statements. The first part introduces the speaker as an immortal, the second part suggests coming without caution, the third part is a polite refusal, and the fourth part questions the need for someone who looks ordinary.
  • 灵气所钟，必凭生物以游人世，不必身即是鹅也 - This sentence explains that the immortal is blessed with spiritual energy and relies on living beings to move among humans, not necessarily being a goose.
  • 世之灵物，修炼成仙，抑又何说 - This sentence questions the idea that spiritual beings become immortals through cultivation.
  • 绸缪年余，忽谓商曰：“妾谪限已满，行与君别。”乃盛妆出门 - This sentence describes the immortal being intimate for over a year, then suddenly telling the speaker that her punishment period is over and she will leave, dressing in fine clothes to depart.
  • 有彩霞万道，从空堕地，层层如丹梯。女踏之，冉冉入云端而没 - This sentence describes a spectacular scene of rainbow-colored clouds falling from the sky, resembling a red ladder, and the immortal gradually disappearing into the clouds.

  • 故相安之 - 故 means 'therefore,' and 相安之 means 'to live together peacefully.' This phrase means that therefore, they lived together peacefully.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 倭某 - Refers to a specific individual, likely a commoner or servant.
  • 邑县署之禁卒也 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'a guard at the county yamen.'
  • 衙前盹睡 - Means 'dozing in front of the yamen.'
  • 梦二役唤之去 - Means 'dreamed of two officials calling him.'
  • 路甚生疏 - Means 'the path was very unfamiliar.'
  • 至一署 - Means 'arrived at an office.'
  • 役引至堂下 - Means 'the officials led him to the hall.'
  • 报勾倭某到 - Means 'reported that倭某 had arrived.'
  • 旋见二囚负锁带械而至 - Means 'soon saw two prisoners bound and shackled arrive.'
  • 审视，即前日狱中新毙之犯也 - Means 'upon inspection, they were the new prisoners who had died in the jail the previous day.'
  • 骇问二役，疑身已死 - Means 'asked the officials in shock, suspecting he was already dead.'
  • 役云：“死否尚不可知，不过对质数言耳。” - Means 'the officials said, 'Whether you are dead or not is still unknown, but it's just a few words of questioning.''
  • 官谓倭曰：“为何虐遇二因，致彼枉死于狱？是非自有官法，尔辈何敢尔？” - Means 'the official said to倭, 'Why did you mistreat the two, causing them to die wrongfully in the jail? Right and wrong have official laws, how dare you do such things?''
  • 对曰：“小人充当禁卒，惟管上退镣铐，并送犯入禁中，他不知也。” - Means 'he replied, 'I am just a guard, responsible only for removing shackles and sending prisoners into the jail, I don't know anything else.''
  • 官使二囚试认 - Means 'the official asked the two prisoners to identify.'
  • 二囚云：“虐我者固非他，然彼未尝不知其人。” - Means 'the prisoners said, 'The one who mistreated us is not him, but he certainly knows the person.''
  • 官诘倭 - Means 'the official questioned倭.'
  • 倭云：“县署差役数百人，轮流充当营务处差，不实指其为谁，小人不敢知也。” - Means '倭 said, 'There are hundreds of officials in the county yamen, taking turns to serve in the camp office, if I don't point out exactly who it is, I cannot know.''
  • 官还问二囚，不能对 - Means 'the official asked the two prisoners again, but they could not answer.'
  • 官曰：“汝辈不能指实，我安能遍勾众役？” - Means 'the official said, 'If you cannot point out exactly, how can I summon all the officials?''
  • 令退，遣倭还阳，一惊而寤 - Means 'ordered them to leave, sent倭 back to the living world, and倭 woke up in a shock.'
  • 后半载，倭始卒 - Means 'half a year later,倭 died.'

  • 邑县署 - Refers to the county yamen, the local government office.
  • 禁卒 - Refers to a guard or jailer.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - A classical way to introduce a speaker, often used in anecdotes or stories.
  • 鬼神恶仆心忍 - Literally 'ghosts and gods hate my heart's cruelty,' but it's an idiom suggesting that the spirits are displeased with the speaker's harshness.
  • 凭马为言以骇之 - Literally 'relying on the horse to speak to frighten him,' meaning the horse is speaking on behalf of the spirits to scare the speaker.
  • 托生 - Refers to rebirth or reincarnation in a different form.
  • 迷魂汤 - A term used in Chinese folklore, referring to a potion that makes one forget their past life.
  • 言为心声 - Literally 'words are the voice of the heart,' meaning that what one says reflects their inner feelings.
  • 忿怒所积，郁极必发 - Literally 'accumulated anger, when it reaches its peak, must be released,' indicating that pent-up anger will eventually manifest.
  • 受挞难忍时 - Literally 'when being beaten becomes unbearable,' referring to a moment of extreme distress.
  • 猝然脱口 - Literally 'suddenly let slip,' meaning to say something without thinking.
  • 声出则气泄 - Literally 'when the sound comes out, the breath is released,' suggesting that speaking relieves tension.
  • 挞止而忿亦平 - Literally 'when the beating stops, the anger also calms down,' indicating that the cessation of physical punishment leads to emotional relief.
  • 无奇 - Literally 'nothing strange,' implying that there is no unusual behavior after the anger subsides.
  • 山左布商 - A geographical and occupational reference, '山左' (Shan Zuo) refers to the area east of Mount Tai (in present-day Shandong Province), and '布商' means 'cloth merchant.'
  • 流落于此 - Literally 'wandering here,' meaning to be in a place by chance or due to circumstances.
  • 苦不能归 - Literally 'suffering from not being able to return,' expressing a desire to go back home but unable to do so.
  • 万生有福 - Literally 'ten thousand lives have good fortune,' a hyperbolic expression indicating great luck.
  • 遣人来迓 - Literally 'send someone to come and meet,' meaning to send someone to pick up the speaker.
  • 感德无穷 - Literally 'infinite gratitude,' expressing deep appreciation.
  • 泪落如雨 - Literally 'tears fall like rain,' a vivid description of crying heavily.
  • 恻然 - Literally 'moved with pity,' indicating a feeling of sympathy.
  • 扪金相赠 - Literally 'touching gold to give as a gift,' meaning to offer money or valuables.
  • 固辞不受 - Literally 'firmly refusing to accept,' indicating a polite but firm refusal.
  • 冒雨入 - Literally 'entering in the rain,' describing someone entering a place despite the rain.
  • 衣如悬鹑 - Literally 'clothes like a sparrow,' a metaphor for tattered or worn-out clothing.
  • 容颜枯瘦 - Literally 'withered and emaciated appearance,' describing a gaunt look.
  • 向客一揖 - Literally 'bowing to the guest,' a gesture of respect.
  • 侧立垂手 - Literally 'standing aside with hands hanging down,' a posture of submission or respect.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鬼神恶仆心忍 - This phrase involves a belief in the supernatural and the idea that spirits can be influenced by human actions.
  • 托生 - This concept is part of Chinese Buddhist and Taoist beliefs about rebirth and reincarnation.
  • 迷魂汤 - This is a folkloric concept found in Chinese mythology, often used in stories and legends.
  • 言为心声 - This is a philosophical concept that emphasizes the connection between words and inner feelings.
  • 忿怒所积，郁极必发 - This reflects traditional Chinese views on emotions and their expression.
  • 山左 - Refers to the eastern part of Shandong Province, historically known for its cultural and economic significance.
  • 布商 - Cloth merchants were important in ancient Chinese society, especially in regions like Shandong.

Proper Nouns

  • 醉茶子 - A nickname or title for the speaker, possibly a fictional character.
  • 山左 - A geographical term referring to the eastern part of Shandong Province.
  • 布商 - An occupational term referring to a cloth merchant.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - A classical introductory phrase meaning 'The Drunken Tea Master says.' '醉茶子' is a playful or poetic title for the speaker, possibly indicating a wise or eccentric figure.
  • 牛知報主，其良不没 - Literally, 'The ox knows to repay its master; its virtue is not forgotten.' This is an idiom from *The Book of Rites* (*Liji*), meaning that even animals have a sense of gratitude and reciprocity.
  • 宜其牛而豕，豕而人也 - Literally, 'It is appropriate for it to be an ox, then a pig, then a human.' This phrase suggests a progression or transformation, possibly implying that humans, like animals, are subject to moral judgment.
  • 颠倒众生，罚不稍恕 - Literally, 'To invert the world, punishment is not slightly lenient.' This is a Buddhist idiom meaning that those who disrupt the natural order or cause suffering will face severe consequences.
  • 昭昭之报，悉冥冥之刑 - Literally, 'Clear rewards are all dark punishments.' This suggests that even seemingly just rewards may ultimately lead to unseen or karmic consequences.
  • 士大夫可不兢兢自爱欤 - Literally, 'Can the scholars and officials not be cautious and self-loving?' This is a rhetorical question urging self-discipline and moral vigilance.
  • 妖诈食 - A playful or satirical title for the second story, possibly indicating a tale of deceit or supernatural elements.
  • 误践一物 - Literally, 'mistakenly stepped on something.'
  • 腻然肥软 - Describes something as greasy, fatty, and soft.
  • 疑其猬也 - Literally, 'suspected it was a hedgehog.'
  • 祝曰 - Means 'prayed' or 'spoke in prayer.'
  • 愚人夜行无炬 - Literally, 'a foolish person walking at night without a torch.'
  • 深自悔惧 - Means 'deeply repentant and fearful.'
  • 敢祈上仙万勿加罪 - Literally, 'dare to pray that the immortal will not impose punishment.'
  • 甫至家门 - Means 'just arrived at the door of the house.'
  • 闻堂中掷器声铮铮然 - Literally, 'heard the sound of objects being thrown in the hall, clanging.'
  • 破瓯残盏 - Refers to broken bowls and cups.
  • 狼藉盈前 - Means 'scattered and overflowing.'
  • 方欲致问 - Means 'was about to ask.'
  • 妻忽瞋目云 - Literally, 'the wife suddenly glared and said.'
  • 无端被凶人作践 - Literally, 'unjustly mistreated by a wicked person.'
  • 使我胁痛骨伤 - Means 'made my ribs hurt and bones ache.'
  • 此怨誓必相报 - Means 'this grudge will surely be avenged.'
  • 家人焚冥镪无算 - Literally, 'the family burned countless paper money.'
  • 其怒稍解 - Means 'her anger slightly subsided.'
  • 明日亟以旨酒嘉肴祀我 - Literally, 'tomorrow, quickly offer fine wine and delicious food to me.'
  • 则涣然冰释 - Means 'then it will be resolved like ice melting.'
  • 否则不尔活也 - Literally, 'otherwise, I will not let you live.'
  • 泯首自赎 - Means 'bowed in self-redemption.'
  • 妇昏睡不醒 - Means 'the wife slept deeply and did not wake.'
  • 携酒榼香楮至旧处设祭 - Literally, 'carried wine, incense, and paper money to the old place to offer a sacrifice.'
  • 视昨所践者 - Means 'looked at what was stepped on yesterday.'
  • 乃一大瓜也 - Literally, 'was a large melon.'
  • 恍然悟悔 - Means 'suddenly realized and regretted.'
  • 痛诋 - Means 'severely criticized.'
  • 妇陡然寒战而愈矣 - Literally, 'the wife suddenly shivered and then recovered.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 牛知报主 - A reference to *The Book of Rites* (*Liji*), emphasizing the moral principle that gratitude and reciprocity are universal, even among animals.
  • 颠倒众生 - A Buddhist concept indicating that those who disrupt the natural order or cause suffering will face severe consequences.
  • 士大夫 - Refers to the scholar-official class in ancient China, who were expected to uphold moral and ethical standards.
  • 妖诈食 - The title suggests a story involving deceit or supernatural elements, possibly drawing on folklore or classical tales of trickery.
  • 冥镪 - Paper money burned as an offering to the dead, a common practice in Chinese funerary rituals.
  • 旨酒嘉肴 - Fine wine and delicious food, often used in offerings or feasts to honor deities or ancestors.

Proper Nouns

  • 醉茶子 - A playful or poetic title for the speaker, possibly indicating a wise or eccentric figure.
  • 邑人 - Refers to a commoner or townsman.
  • 冥镪 - Paper money burned as an offering to the dead.
  • 旨酒嘉肴 - Fine wine and delicious food, often used in offerings or feasts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 邑 - Refers to a small town or county.
  • 除日 - Refers to the eve of the New Year, a time of celebration.
  • 教军厂 - Refers to a military training ground or barracks.
  • 东厢 - Refers to the eastern wing of a building, often used as a place of solitude or seclusion.
  • 窗上投缳 - Refers to the act of hanging oneself from a window, a traditional method of suicide.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 落魄奇穷，无以卒岁 - This phrase describes the man's extreme poverty and inability to survive the year.
  • 自顾其室则嗷嗷众口，坐以待毙 - This clause emphasizes the man's despair and the dire situation of his family.
  • 身为男子，不能致富，使妻孥困惫至此，不如死之为佳 - This sentence expresses the man's frustration and his belief that death is preferable to his current state.
  • 浩叹而出，信步至教军厂 - This sequence describes the man's emotional state and his aimless wandering.
  • 见其东厢内僻静无人，思于窗上投缳，俾得死所 - This clause outlines the man's plan to commit suicide.
  • 踌躇已定，归求助于亲友，凡历数家，井无有假一文者 - This sequence shows the man's attempt to seek help and his ultimate failure.
  • 心灰志丧，而念愈果 - This phrase indicates the man's growing resolve despite his despair.
  • 入夜，嗒然竟往，至东厢，漆漆然黑不见物 - This sequence describes the man's journey to the eastern wing at night.
  • 急趋窗下，解带将悬，忽有物触手，扪之，人也，高挂棂际，盖缢死矣 - This final sequence describes the man's attempt to hang himself and the discovery of a body.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence is structured to contrast the expected magical quality with the unexpected foolishness, emphasizing the latter.
  • This clause sets up a hypothetical scenario where one cannot distinguish between beauty and ugliness, leading to the paradoxical statement about Zhang.
  • This is a philosophical statement that requires careful rendering to maintain the Buddhist concept of interdependence.
  • This proverb is a metaphor for the transient nature of wealth and fortune.
  • This sentence is complex, detailing the mother's actions and her instructions to her son.
  • This sequence describes the rapid decline of Zhu's life, emphasizing his isolation and poverty.
  • This describes Zhu's discovery of the buried treasure, which turns out to be disappointing.
  • The final sentence summarizes Zhu's tragic end.

  • 无盐 - Wu Yan, a famous ugly woman in Chinese history.
  • 朱某 - Zhu, the main character in the story.
  • 白镪 - Silver, a valuable metal.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sequence '爰及農商工賈，廝役奴隸，釣魚屠肉，飯牛牧羊' must be read as a single catalogue of social groups and occupations.
  • The clause '皆有先達，可為師表' states that all of the listed groups have worthy predecessors who may serve as models.
  • The closing clause '博學求之，無不利於事也' links broad learning from such people to practical benefit in action.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 焉有嫁不及时，遽来强暴之辱 - Rhetorical question: How could there be a situation where a woman is forced into an unwanted marriage so soon after her marriageable age? Combines suddenness ('遽') with forced marriage ('强暴之辱').
  • 如晨钟暮鼓 - Metaphor for sudden enlightenment or awakening.
  • 漏尽 - Sound of the night watchman's bell, indicating the end of the night.
  • 妖由人兴 - Belief that evil comes from human actions.
  • 函丈 - Space between the teacher and student during a lesson, symbolizing respect and distance.
  • 盖阳虽规诫，而阴怀忌妒 - Although he appeared to be advising him, he secretly harbored jealousy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 嫁不及时 - Refers to the traditional practice of arranging marriages for young women, often at a young age.
  • 强暴之辱 - Refers to the forced marriage of a woman, which was a serious social issue in ancient China.
  • 贞信自守 - Refers to a woman's virtue and self-restraint, which were highly valued in traditional Chinese society.
  • 二南 - Refers to the regions of Nan (Southern) Zhou and Nan (Southern) Yan, known for their cultural refinement and moral values.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The rhetorical question '焉有嫁不及时，遽来强暴之辱' combines the idea of suddenness with the concept of forced marriage.
  • The phrase '遂令贞信自守者以此为可惧' links the forced marriage to the fear it instills in virtuous women.
  • The comparison '吾想极乱之世尚不致如此，况二南被化之国乎' emphasizes the improbability of the situation.
  • The clause '此等处可以不必死于句下也' suggests that the situation does not warrant extreme punishment.
  • The metaphorical description '生闻言，如晨钟暮鼓，入耳豁然，遂悦服遵教' is followed by the young man's willingness to follow the teachings.
  • The sequence of events '漏尽女自去，生始就枕' requires careful translation to maintain the temporal order.
  • The description '如是年余，生文思大进，塾师不能易一字' shows the young man's improvement in literary talent.
  • The surprise of the classmates is shown in '同学者共猜之，然不料其有是也'.
  • The classmate's discovery is described in '一夜，有同学生起溺，闻生室中有人声，舐窗私窥，见生孤坐灯下，言犹未已'.
  • The classmate's reaction and the young man's confession are described in '叩扃穷诘，生不能隐，具以实告'.
  • The classmate's advice is structured as a series of rhetorical questions and statements: '妖由人兴，不早与绝，是自求祸。伊谁见古来名儒，有受业于狐者？学品果优，避之不暇，况公然敢犯函丈而居师位乎？'
  • The final clause '盖阳虽规诫，而阴怀忌妒' reveals the classmate's true feelings.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '第恐不能自专，归烦禀命尊人' is a compound structure where the first part expresses a fear, and the second part explains the consequence.
  • The sentence '如其协也，予择吉送女去' is a conditional sentence where the condition is followed by the action to be taken if the condition is met.
  • The sentence '实告君，我辈狐仙也，与君有前缘焉' is a compound sentence where the first part introduces the speaker's identity, and the second part explains the connection.
  • The sentence '区区之意，姥所深知' is a simple sentence where the first part expresses the speaker's intention, and the second part confirms that the grandmother is aware of it.
  • The sentence '蓄有一物，归而献之，为言求婚，尊人必愿' is a compound sentence where the first part describes what the speaker has, the second part explains what will be done with it, and the third part predicts the outcome.
  • The sentence '如有回音，可于某日俟我于城东之石桥' is a conditional sentence where the condition is followed by the action to be taken if the condition is met.
  • The sentence '乃出一绢，包缄固甚密，赠生藏诸怀' is a series of actions performed by the speaker, each described in a separate clause.
  • The sentence '导生出门，嘱云：“所约之期勿忘也。”' is a compound sentence where the first part describes the action of guiding someone out, and the second part includes a speech.
  • The sentence '返身遂去' is a simple sentence describing the action of turning around and leaving.
  • The sentence '生视之，松楸夹路，并无居庐' is a compound sentence where the first part describes the action of looking, and the second part describes what was seen.
  • The sentence '实告其父，不敢隐' is a compound sentence where the first part describes the action of telling the truth, and the second part explains the reason.
  • The sentence '启其缄，重纸包裹，中一铜尺' is a series of actions performed by the speaker, each described in a separate clause.
  • The sentence '其铭云：“尺非长，寸非短，宜子孙，垂久远。”' is a direct quotation of the inscription on the copper ruler.
  • The sentence '洵汉代物，不禁咤异' is a compound sentence where the first part confirms the artifact's origin, and the second part expresses the speaker's reaction.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 次子 - The second son, referring to the second son in the family.

  • 长者 - The elder, referring to an older family member, likely the father or another authority figure.
  • 汝年已老，何乐于生 - A rhetorical question used to pressure the mother into compliance, reflecting a common method of manipulation in traditional Chinese society.
  • 如此一死，棺殓丰富，快何如之 - Another rhetorical statement used to manipulate the mother, emphasizing the perceived benefits of death.
  • 母觳觫乞免 - Describes the mother's fear and trembling, a common physical reaction to extreme stress or fear.

  • 而岳家因是拖累，家亦落 - The son-in-law's family suffering as a result, reflecting the broader social and familial consequences of such incidents.
  • 三子藁葬其母，事亦遂寝 - The simple burial of the mother and the eventual forgetting of the matter, reflecting the social and emotional impact on the family.
  • 按冥律 - The laws of the underworld, reflecting traditional Chinese beliefs about the afterlife and moral judgment.
  • 三子行同枭獍 - The three sons behaving like vultures and wolves, reflecting the severity of their actions and the moral judgment of the text.
  • 宜凌迟处死 - Execution by slicing, reflecting the severity of the punishment for such crimes.
  • 来生五世为豕，受宰割刑 - Punishment in the afterlife, reflecting traditional Chinese beliefs about karma and reincarnation.
  • 宰贪贿枉法 - Corruption in the legal system, reflecting the potential for abuse of power and justice.
  • 宜肢解 - Dismemberment, reflecting the severity of the punishment for corruption.
  • 一起畿南许姓，拐卖良家童男女二十二名口，未经破案 - A case of kidnapping and selling children, reflecting the prevalence of such crimes in traditional Chinese society and the limitations of the legal system in addressing them.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 昭昭之报，即冥冥之刑 - Philosophical statement suggesting that clear rewards are equivalent to hidden punishments, implying a deeper moral or karmic logic.
  • 理固可信也 - Means 'the principle is certainly credible,' affirming the reliability of the preceding statement.
  • 宋儒谓鬼神无非阴阳屈伸之理 - Statement about Song dynasty Confucian scholars' view that the existence of ghosts and gods is nothing more than the natural principles of yin and yang, and their expansion and contraction.
  • 驳释氏轮回之说 - Refers to the rejection of the Buddhist doctrine of reincarnation.
  • 云人死为鬼，鬼又为人，如此生生不已，造物无权矣 - Statement arguing that if people become ghosts upon death and then return to human form, this endless cycle implies that the creator has no authority.
  • 羊祜探环，非熊再世 - Historical allusions to Yang Hu and Jiang Shang, suggesting that these figures' fates or legacies were reborn or repeated.
  • 显有明证，岂尽诬耶 - Rhetorical question implying that there are clear evidences, so it cannot be entirely false.
  • 不知理虽非正，事总非虚，岂即置而勿论乎 - Rhetorical question implying that even if the principle is not correct, the events are not entirely false, so they should not be dismissed without discussion.
  • 宋儒之迂拘，往往类此，真不足一噱 - Statement criticizing Song dynasty Confucian scholars for their narrow-mindedness and suggesting that such criticism is not worthy of serious consideration.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋儒 - Refers to scholars of the Song dynasty, particularly those associated with Neo-Confucianism.
  • 鬼神 - Refers to ghosts and gods, which are central to Chinese folk beliefs and religious practices.
  • 阴阳屈伸之理 - Refers to the principles of yin and yang, and their expansion and contraction, which are fundamental concepts in Chinese philosophy.
  • 释氏轮回之说 - Refers to the Buddhist doctrine of reincarnation, which was a significant point of contention between Confucian and Buddhist thought in China.
  • 羊祜 - A historical figure from the Three Kingdoms period, known for his military and political achievements.
  • 非熊 - Refers to Jiang Shang (also known as Jiang Ziya), a legendary figure who served King Wen of Zhou.
  • 史传所载 - Refers to historical records and accounts, emphasizing the importance of documented evidence.
  • 造物 - Refers to the creator or the universe, often used in philosophical and religious contexts.
  • 迂拘 - Refers to narrow-mindedness or rigidity in thought.
  • 一噱 - A colloquial term meaning 'a joke' or 'something not worthy of serious consideration.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鲁班 - Refers to Lu Ban, a legendary figure in Chinese folklore known as the father of Chinese technology and a master craftsman.

  • 康熙年间 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Kangxi (1661-1722) during the Qing dynasty, a period of significant cultural and technological development.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 醉茶子曰 - Classical form of attribution, indicating the text is attributed to a symbolic figure named 'Drunken Tea Child.'
  • 聽其谲詞，遽增其壽 - Listening to his deceptive words, suddenly increasing his lifespan; suggests deception leading to false hope.
  • 神幾近悠悠矣 - The divine is becoming distant or unclear; suggests a loss of clarity or connection to the divine.
  • 言為心聲 - Words are the voice of the heart; emphasizes the connection between speech and inner thoughts.
  • 侃侃之論 - Earnest or straightforward discourse; describes sincere and forceful speech.
  • 剃發者之口 - The mouth of a shaved person; historically refers to a Buddhist monk.
  • 無是心者無是言也 - One who lacks a certain heart will not speak in that way; emphasizes the connection between inner disposition and outer expression.
  • 善則必賞，所以勸惡 - Good deeds must be rewarded, to encourage the wicked; suggests that rewarding good deeds serves as a deterrent to evil.
  • 神豈妄哉 - How could the divine be false?; emphasizes the reliability of the divine.
  • 蜈蚣 - Common term for centipede, but in this context, refers to a large, mythical centipede.
  • 邑西南村郊外 - Outskirts of the southwest village of the town; indicates the location of the event.
  • 出沒不測 - Appearing and disappearing unpredictably; describes the centipede’s mysterious behavior.
  • 夜則有光如炬 - At night it has a light like a torch; describes the centipede’s glowing appearance.
  • 照灼數步 - Illuminating several steps; indicates the extent of the centipede’s light.
  • 村人患之 - The villagers were troubled by it; indicates that the centipede caused distress.
  • 集眾持梃冥搜 - Gathered people and searched with sticks in the dark; describes the villagers’ efforts to find the centipede.
  • 盤伏如帶 - Coiled and lying like a belt; describes the centipede’s posture.
  • 夭矫難制 - Twisting and difficult to control; describes the centipede’s movements.
  • 二健夫奮力亂擊始毙 - Two strong men struck it with all their might until it died; describes the villagers’ successful attempt to kill the centipede.
  • 歸覺肢体麻術 - They returned and felt their limbs numb; indicates the physical effects of the centipede’s poison.
  • 視其二人之臂，均黑肿如墨 - When they looked at the arms of the two men, they were both black and swollen like ink; describes the severe effects of the centipede’s poison.
  • 物最毒，雖未着手，而从梃递及 - The creature is the most poisonous, even though it did not touch them, its poison spread through the sticks; explains how the poison affected the villagers.
  • 怪物不可以力敵也 - Monsters cannot be fought with brute force; emphasizes the danger of underestimating supernatural or mythical creatures.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 醉茶子 - Fictional or symbolic figure, likely representing a wise or eccentric individual; retain the name in translation.
  • 剃發者 - Historically refers to Buddhist monks, as they shave their heads as part of their religious practice.
  • 蜈蚣 - Commonly refers to centipedes, but in this context, likely refers to a mythical or supernatural centipede, given its size and glowing appearance.
  • 邑西南村郊外 - Refers to a specific location in the outskirts of a town, emphasizing the rural setting of the story.

Proper Nouns

  • 醉茶子 - Retain the name as it is, as it is a symbolic figure.
  • 蜈蚣 - Retain the term as it is, as it refers to a specific creature in the story.
